Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'm 29, working part-time and dealing with a lot of life stuff, so I couldn't really set a consistent schedule. I'd usually grab time whenever I could, often late at night or during breaks at work. I learn best by doing things with my hands, so I'd try to work through problems physically, writing things out on paper rather than just reading on a screen. The 13 hours a week I managed felt scattered across random pockets of time.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines and whatever crisis was happening that week. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, then I'd have these intense bursts when assignments were due. I missed a lot of classes—probably showed up less than half the time—so I was always playing catch-up. The irregularity stressed me out, but I couldn't seem to break the cycle no matter how much I wanted to.
Q3: Usually it was panic about an assignment deadline that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd get a notification about something and that would remind me the course existed. Without regular internet at home, I had to plan my LMS time around when I could get to the library or borrow someone's connection. It wasn't a natural habit for me; it always felt like an effort to remember to check in.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The gamified tasks they introduced were kind of fun and made me want to engage more toward the end, which actually put me in a better mood about the whole thing. But I never got to that point where logging in felt automatic. Without consistent internet access, building any kind of digital routine was nearly impossible for me.
Q5: The gamified activities were actually the most useful for me because they made learning feel active rather than passive. I could earn points and see progress, which appealed to how I like to learn by doing. The discussion posts were okay too because I could interact with other students and that social element helped things stick. Watching videos was hit or miss—over two hours of them—but only when I could actually focus.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the extra reading materials and optional resources. Without reliable internet, downloading or streaming anything extra felt like a luxury I couldn't afford time-wise. Some of the discussion threads got really long and I'd just skim them instead of reading deeply. If something wasn't directly tied to an assignment grade, it usually fell to the bottom of my priority list.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see an immediate connection to my assignments or if it was hands-on in some way. The gamified elements really helped because earning badges or points gave me that instant feedback. I also valued anything I could do offline after downloading, since that fit my actual life circumstances. Sitting through passive content without interaction felt like wasted time I didn't have.
Q8: Required activities got done because they had to—that's why my assignment completion was so high even though I was struggling otherwise. Optional stuff felt like a luxury for students with more time and better circumstances than me. I'd look at optional activities and think 'that would be nice' but then reality would hit. There was always this guilt about not doing more, but I had to prioritize survival over enrichment.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my real studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs and print them out at the library, then work through problems by hand at home or during my commute. I used a lot of handwritten notes and would physically work through concepts because that's how things click for me. My textbook got way more attention than the LMS ever showed—I'd have it open for hours that nobody tracked.
Q10: So many times. I'd spend entire evenings working through printed materials, rewriting notes, and practicing problems on paper. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd also study with a classmate sometimes, talking through concepts together, which was incredibly helpful but completely invisible. My exam scores didn't reflect how much time I actually put in offline, which was frustrating.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd log in once, download everything I needed for the week, and then work through it all offline over the next several days. My login frequency was pretty low compared to how much I was actually engaging with the materials. It was a survival strategy given my internet situation. One download session might fuel three or four days of actual studying.
Q12: There was a period mid-semester when my anxiety was through the roof and I just couldn't focus on anything. Work got more demanding, and I was dealing with some personal stuff that made it hard to even think about school. My attendance dropped even more during that time. The stress made everything feel overwhelming, and the course materials started to feel like they were written in another language.
Q13: Definitely. Fatigue was a big one—after working, I'd be too exhausted to engage meaningfully with anything online. Confusion about course material made me avoid the LMS because logging in just reminded me how lost I felt. When work picked up or family stuff demanded attention, the course was the first thing to get pushed aside. My LMS usage was basically a barometer of how overwhelmed I was with everything else.
Q14: Oh, all the time. I'd have videos playing while I was half-asleep or distracted by other things. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really absorbing anything, just going through the motions. There were sessions where I'd be logged in for an hour but couldn't tell you a single thing I learned. My mind would be elsewhere, worrying about bills or work or whatever else was weighing on me.
Q15: I definitely avoided exam prep even though I knew it was crucial—my exam score shows that clearly. The anxiety made it hard to face material I didn't fully understand, so I'd focus on assignments instead because those felt more manageable. I'd tell myself I'd study for exams 'tomorrow' and tomorrow never came. It was easier to complete concrete tasks than to face the open-ended terror of exam preparation.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to engage consistently. My assignment completion was really high because deadlines created urgency I couldn't ignore. I'd often be working right up until the last minute, which was stressful but effective for getting things submitted. Without a deadline, I'd drift and procrastinate indefinitely. The structure of due dates was like a lifeline in my chaotic schedule.
Q17: When I got good feedback on assignments, it actually boosted my mood and made me want to engage more. My assignment scores were really strong, so that positive reinforcement helped counter some of my anxiety. But feedback on exams was discouraging and made me want to avoid that whole area even more. It created this weird split where I felt capable in some areas and completely hopeless in others.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but it assumed students had consistent access and regular schedules, which I didn't have. The gamified elements they added were actually really helpful for someone like me who needs active engagement. But the heavy reliance on online participation penalized my situation. I would have thrived with more hands-on, in-person components and less assumption that everyone could just log in whenever.
Q19: The gamification made engagement easier because it gave me concrete goals and rewards, which matched how I like to learn. What made it harder was the assumption that everyone had reliable internet and could participate in real-time discussions. The exam-heavy grading structure was brutal for me since my strengths were in applied work, not test-taking. More flexibility in how we could demonstrate learning would have helped enormously.
Q20: I think those students might be like me—logging in, downloading things, maybe starting activities but not finishing them online. They could be anxious or overwhelmed, clicking around without actually submitting because nothing feels good enough. Or they might be doing the real work offline and just not uploading it. High activity without submissions often signals struggle, not laziness.
Q21: That actually does reflect my experience in some ways. My assignment scores were excellent even though my LMS engagement looked low, because I was doing serious work offline. Some students just don't need to be constantly logged in to learn effectively. If you're a kinesthetic learner like me, you might get more from physical practice than from clicking through online modules. The LMS doesn't capture everything.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—like when someone crams before deadlines or stops engaging entirely. They're good at tracking submissions and basic participation metrics. But they completely miss offline studying, emotional state, life circumstances, and the quality of engagement versus just time spent. Someone could be logged in for hours and learn nothing, or study intensely offline and look disengaged online.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm actively thinking, questioning, and trying to apply what I'm learning. For me, it happens most when I'm physically working through problems or discussing ideas with someone. It's a mental and emotional investment that doesn't always correlate with how much time I spend logged into a system.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much anxiety I was carrying every time I thought about this course, or how my lack of internet access shaped everything I did. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with printed materials or the study sessions with classmates. It definitely wouldn't show that despite failing, I actually learned a lot and my mood improved by the end because of the gamified approach. My engagement was real—it just didn't fit neatly into their tracking system.
